text: Hello friends, this is a case of laparoscopic fundoplication and this patient has grade III GERD.
The surgeon inserts a trocar through a supraumbilical port, located approximately five centimeters above the umbilicus, following the completion of insufflation.

The surgeon positions two laparoscopic ports: one in the midclavicular line approximately five centimeters below the costal margin for the right hand, and another in the midclavicular line just below the costal margin on the right side for the left hand.

The surgeon positions an epigastric port just below the xiphoid process and to the left of the falciform ligament, intended for use with the Nathanson liver retractor. Additional ports are placed in the left and right hypochondrium, with the left side positioned five centimeters below the costal margin and the right side just below the costal margin.
The surgeon introduces the Nathanson liver retractor percutaneously under the telescope's vision, lifting the left lobe of the liver to provide exposure for the laparoscopic fundoplication procedure.

The surgeon uses the left hand to hold the stomach while the assistant holds the short gastric vessels. They begin separating the vessels from the stomach, freeing the fundus to mobilize it for a floppy fundus and achieve the sunshine effect necessary for a proper wrap.
The surgeon ensures a tension-free wrap by separating the short gastric vessels, allowing for adequate mobilization of the stomach fundus.
The assistant port is positioned on the left side, along the anterior axillary line, 7.5 centimeters lateral and below the left hypochondriac port.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel in the right hand and an atraumatic grasper in the left hand to lift the stomach while the assistant supports the omentum. The harmonic scalpel is used to separate the omentum, aiming to reach the left crus.

The surgical instruments reach near the diaphragm, where the spleen is visible above. The surgeon carefully separates and mobilizes the tissue near the spleen, reaching up to the left crus of the diaphragm.

The surgeon separates the peritoneum over the left crus while applying medial and inferior traction on the stomach. This maneuver stretches the peritoneum, preventing injury to the esophagus, and allows mobilization through the angle of His. The anterior peritoneum is visible during this process.

The surgeon reaches the right side up to the pars flaccida, identifying the muscle of the left crus. The pars flaccida is visible, and the surgeon begins opening the lesser sac, followed by anterior mobilization of the esophagus.
and then they go towards the angle of his. This is another way to do it. But both are same. You may start from the right and then go to the left or start from the left and go to the right.

The caudate lobe of the liver is visible, with the opened lesser sac below it. The surgeon carefully navigates near the vena cava, which is located beneath the caudate lobe, while identifying the right crus of the diaphragm.

The surgeon widens the opening of the pars flaccida and begins lateral mobilization, performing dissection between the right crus and the esophagus. The peritoneum is opened, and blunt dissection is used to create a posterior window.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to mobilize approximately five centimeters of the esophagus, carefully separating it posteriorly and anteriorly from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the anterior vagus nerve embedded in the esophagus while ensuring the posterior vagus nerve remains with the esophagus during posterior mobilization.

The surgeon carefully avoids entering the mediastinum while ensuring the dissection plane does not injure surrounding structures. The vagus nerve is visible and is gently mobilized and pushed alongside the esophagus.

The surgeon identifies the left crus of the diaphragm in front of the posterior mediastinum, proceeding with mobilization from the left side. The anterior vagus nerve is visible, embedded in the esophagus, as anterior mobilization continues around the left crus and esophagus.

The surgeon has freed the left crus from the esophagus and begins creating a window on the right side. Anterior mobilization is performed with appropriate anterolateral and anteromedial traction on the stomach.
The surgeon hooks the oesophagus anterolaterally, followed by posterior mobilization to further free the oesophagus from surrounding tissues.

A window is formed around the posterior aspect of the oesophagus, with the posterior vagus nerve visible and being gently pushed towards the oesophagus. The surgeon proceeds to enlarge the window for further mobilization.

The surgeon enlarges the posterior window, revealing the fundus of the stomach. Using a harmonic scalpel, they cut fibrous trabeculae posteriorly, ensuring careful avoidance of the posterior oesophageal vessel to prevent bleeding. Blunt dissection is employed to further mobilize the oesophagus and expand the posterior window.
The surgeon uses a grasper to pull the posterior part of the fundus from right to left, preparing to wrap it around the esophagus as part of the fundoplication procedure.

The surgeon demonstrates the shoe shine effect with the floppy fundus, pulling the posterior part of the fundus and handing it to the assistant. The assistant uses an atraumatic grasper to hold the fundus, while a small area of stomach serosa appears slightly damaged.
The surgeon performs crural approximation by taking a suture bite from right to left across the hiatus hernia, ensuring the vena cava is not accidentally pricked during the process.

The surgeon takes a suture bite far lateral and far medial, ensuring it remains under vision and does not extend below the caudate lobe. A small hiatal hernia is present, requiring only a single knot, which is tied without over-tightening. The crural approximation is performed with enough space left between the esophagus and the knot to allow a 5mm instrument to pass into the mediastinum.

The surgeon performs crural approximation using a single knot, taking a bite from the right to the left crura. A simple surgeon's knot is tied, starting with a double wrap followed by two alternating wraps, to secure the approximation.

The assistant continuously pulls the fundus while the surgeon performs the plication. The seromuscular layer of the stomach is grasped on either side for plication, using non-absorbable Ethibond No. II sutures. The plication spans three to five centimeters, secured with two or three knots.
The surgeon ties the first knot and prepares to take a second bite one centimeter below the initial knot, using non-absorbable sutures for the plication.

The surgeon optionally takes a bite involving the esophageal muscle or surrounding fat, ensuring the needle does not prick the esophagus. Alternatively, the fundus is sutured to the fundus or stomach to stomach in the Nissen fundoplication procedure.
On the right side of the fundus wrap, a small serosal tear is visible, which will be sutured later using a surgeon's knot.
Thank you.
The surgeon places the third and final suture knot in the fundoplication, completing the plication of the seromuscular layer of the stomach using Ethibond No. II non-absorbable sutures.
The surgeon approximates the small serosal defect on the right side of the fundus wrap, burying the exposed mucosa to ensure a smooth extension of the tissue.

The surgeon completes the Nissen fundoplication procedure, checking for hemostasis. The Nathanson liver retractor and all surgical ports are removed, followed by the evacuation of gas from the abdominal cavity.
